title: CRAM is Universal | Stacey Gabriel
text:
With the CRAM
file format
requiring only
about a third
of the data
storage space
as would have
been required before
we're able to use less space so we save about
two thirds of our of our data storage costs as
we transition to this file format.
And it becomes absolutely critical that that
data is in a format that can be easily
and effectively shared amongst many
investigators.
In other words, it really becomes very wasteful if an investigator has to go and reprocess or reformat
the data every time they get a new data set
in order to perform an analysis.
Just like we have made CRAMs the standardized
file format for large NIH consortium
like the Center for Common Disease Research
or the Center for Mendelian Genetics
previous large projects we've been
involved with in the past.
When we turn to new programs, such as the All of Us
Research Program, what we find is we
already have done a lot of that work to
settle on a standardized file format
and that will be the CRAM file as well that
will be used really systematically
across the three genome centers as we
produce that data for the All of Us program.
